title: Breaking Bipartite and Time Reversal Symmetries by Fusing Porphine Unit in-between two Zigzag-edge Graphene Nanoribbons
authors: R. K. Rohit, Jisvin Sam, Sudipta Dutta
abstract: Hybrid structure of two zigzag-edge graphene nanoribbons with a fused porphine ring in between, results in two distinct nearly degenerate ground states: a semiconducting antiferromagnetic state and a conducting ferromagnetic state with unequal and opposite Fermi velocities of majority and minority spins, the former having slightly higher stability. Such ground states result from the broken bipartite symmetry induced by the porphine ring. The incorporation of different transition metal atoms in the porphine cavity reduces their energy difference but keeps their electronic properties mostly unchanged. The splitting of the $d$-orbitals in the distorted square-planar ligand field of porphine produces a high spin ground state that breaks the global time reversal symmetry ($\mathcal{T}$). The opposite Fermi velocities of the majority and minority spins in the ferromagnetic ground state and lower sensitivity of the conducting majority spin channel to the edge disorder, make this class of quasi-one-dimensional hybrid structures promising for dual spin-filtering device applications.
summary: This theoretical study examines graphene nanoribbons fused with porphine units to engineer specific electronic and magnetic properties. By breaking bipartite and time-reversal symmetries, the system can stabilize a ferromagnetic ground state with distinct majority and minority spin channels. This dual spin-filtering capability makes the structure highly promising for advanced spintronic applications.
main_problem: The study investigates how fusing a porphine unit between two zigzag-edge graphene nanoribbons modifies their electronic and magnetic properties by breaking bipartite and time-reversal symmetries.
main_result: The hybrid structure exhibits two nearly degenerate ground states: a semiconducting antiferromagnetic state and a conducting ferromagnetic state. The FM state is promising for dual spin-filtering due to opposite Fermi velocities for majority and minority spins.
method: Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations, employing the GGA+U formalism for transition metal atoms, were used to analyze the electronic structure.
model_or_system: The physical system is a hybrid structure formed by fusing a porphine ring (Por) between two Zigzag-edge Graphene Nanoribbons (ZGNRs), denoted as $m m’Z-Por$. Substitutions with transition metals (V, Fe, Cu) are also studied.
key_observables: Ground state energy difference ($\Delta$), net magnetization ($\mu_B$), spin-polarized band structures, Density of States (DOS), and spin-resolved Fermi velocities.
important_parameters: ZGNR width, presence/type of transition metal dopant (V, Fe, Cu), and the symmetry breaking induced by the porphine ring.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on a mean-field description for interactions and assumes the porphine ligand geometry dictates the d-orbital splitting pattern.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate spin densities, spin-polarized band structures, and pDOS for various hybrid states (AFM vs. FM), detailing spin localization and orbital hybridization effects.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the electronic structure of the $m m’Z-Por$ system, comparing the AFM and FM ground states. It then details the effect of incorporating transition metals (V, Fe, Cu) and varying the ZGNR width, confirming the symmetry breaking mechanism and its implications for spin transport.
why_interesting: The explicit control over spin-dependent electronic properties via molecular engineering (porphine fusion) and symmetry breaking is highly relevant to designing next-generation spintronic devices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic of mm’Z-Por hybrid structure with fused porphine molecules in between two ZGNR segments at the top and at the bottom, with their widths marked by the numeric values, m and m’, respectively, that are the numbers of the zigzag chains along the y-axis. The inner edges are marked by 1 and 1’ and the outer edges are marked by m and m’. The super-cell is marked with the square bracket. The ground state spin densities of (b) AFM and (c) FM ground states of 33’Z-Por hybrid show different types of spin localiza- tions, namely majority (red) and minority (blue) spins on the two sublattices of the bipartite ZGNR segments. The AFM ground state shows two outer edges, i.e., m and m’...

FIG. 2. Spin polarized k-resolved projected band structures for (a) AFM and (b) FM ground states of 33’Z-Por hybrid. Left and right panels show minority and majority spin band dispersions. The middle panels show spin polarized density of states (DOS) with blue and red shades representing minor- ity and majority spins, respectively. (c) and (d) depict the probability density distributions corresponding to the states marked in boxes in the minority and majority spin band dis- persions of FM state. These two states correspond to the k-point where the minority and majority spin bands cross the Fermi energy. FIG. 3. Spin densities of the 33’Z-(V)Por hybrid in (a) AFM and (d) FM ground states. The majority and minority spins are depicted in red and blue colors, respectively with V atom localizing the minority spin. The corresponding spin resolved band structures for these states are shown in (b) and (e), respectively with same color code. The bands orginating from the V d-orbitals are marked with arrows. Two boxes in (e) indicate the location of the k-points for which the wave-functions of the majority and minority spin bands at the Fermi energy are presented in Fig. S9(a) and (b), respectively.
